sentences.text: At the health problems?
I can't take care, because my sister, she helped me a lot.
My father's youngest sister, she helped me a lot.
And my father's youngest sister, she was-- I-- I talked to you before about that.
She was in the partisan company what's to help kids to go out from ghetto.
They open a special school for kids.
And we was learning Lithuanian to talk correct in Lithuanian.
Because before-- this was in 1942 and 1943-- kids, they was in Lithuanian families.
And the Lithuanian polizei, they find out the kids, they can't talk in-- in Lithuanian, and they kill the kids.
And they kill the Lithuanian families.
And then they start to teach us to talk correct and be very careful.
I was learning about one year Lithuanian.
I was reading a lot in Lithuanian.
And in 1944, in the beginning, my aunt, she took me to a Lithuanian family.
And this family, they help me to survive.
And I stayed there in this family to 1994, in the end, when the Russia army, they-- [NON-ENGLISH]?
They liberated.
Liberated this small town where I was, in Lithuania.
I was stayed there about six months.
But this was very difficult when I was there, because a lot of people, they notice I am a kid from another-- from another family.
And a lot of Lithuanians, they will find out from what family, from what town I came, and-- but I was spoking very nicely in Lithuanian.
And my Lithuanian family, they did not have problem about me.
But they survive four kids.
The partisan-- the-- the company-- the partisan-- what they help 32 kids-- girls, only girls.
Because boys, they can't help them.
This was difficult for boys, to help them.
This family-- this name from the family was Juza Federavicius and Jonas Federavicius.
This was a husband and a wife.
They was-- was very nice to us, to the kids.
They was like a father and a mother.
And in this meantime, I called them mother and father.
And after, when we was [NON-ENGLISH].
Liberated.
Liberated, we was like a family.
They did not never have kids.
And the four kids, was they liberated, they was very warm to them.
And we was warm to-- to this family, to the Lithuanian.
Yes, they do.
They help with us with food, with clothes, with-- to survived.
I can't understand this-- this--
No.
Not in Lithuania.
They was working.
People thinking it was a help-- the-- the Germans.
But it's not true.
Because I know-- I know exactly.
Because my aunt, she was in this partisan company.
And always, the Jewish polizei, they help the-- the-- the partisan to-- they tell them before what to do, because they-- to not to be like in-- how can I-- in [NON-ENGLISH].
In a hole.
In a hole.
In a hole.
Yes.
Yes.
We have a couple people, were they-- fighting with the Germans.
They shooting in the Germans.
One man was named [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember only the name.
And he was killed a couple Germans soldiers.
And I-- I remember one other two Lithuanian polizei, and they--
Hanged him.
Hang him.
And he would stay six days.
And the Germans and the Lithuanian polizei, they-- everybody they talked to-- to the Jewish people-- go and see what we do when you will shoot in Germans other in Lithuanian polizei.
I don't know.
I don't know.
Nobody knows.
Yes, they helped the people in the ghetto.
When the Germans came in-- in ghetto with buses to take people to killed, they was talking to everybody.
Go, go and [NON-ENGLISH].
Hide, because maybe you can survived.
This-- this is expect to you what you can do for yourself.
Any schools, but we have a school the same like in Hebrew, for-- for boys.
And the-- the kids, they are the same.
They have the same problem to look out.
It's not the Germans.
It's not the soldiers.
It's not the Lithuanian polizei.
And the kids, they like to-- to learning and to-- to remember.
And every Friday, they have in the synagogue, the same-- the same serve the-- the Jewish-- the Jewish religion about Friday, about Saturday.
And everybody do what he can do.
This was special apartment.
Somebody tell them, OK, today we-- you came to my apartment.
Tomorrow we came to yours apartment.
And every day, somebody-- they-- they change the places.
We did not have one place, special for the boys.
But the Lithuanian, for the kids, we have a special place, one place only.
They have a lot of children, but the mothers, they never-- like when the kids get out of sight.
The kids was always in sight.
Because kids-- the soldiers-- what they was watching the ghetto around.
They-- sometimes there was shoot the kids.
Others, they grabbed the kids.
And they killed them.
The ghetto was closed.
They have a special-- like a-- a big door.
And when you go out from one side, it's the Jewish polizei.
From the other side, it's the Lithuanian polizei.
One are the two German soldiers.
And when the-- somebody can text-- understand Lithuanian, the Jewish polizei, they explain to them what to do.
This was, in the beginning, when the Germans started the war.
This was in 1941.
A lot of Lithuanian people-- I don't-- I can't say-- they was like bandits.
They killed a lot of rabbis.
But they-- they--
--cut them-- the heads.
And the head, they took on the garbage bags.
And they was laughing.
And they talked to-- to people, look what he can now teach you.
This is--
Yes, a lot of people-- a lot of organization from the partisan, and families, Lithuanian families, where they carried off.
They know this was like friend before the war.
They help them.
And from the organization, kids-- we was 32 kids.
We was survived.
This is thanks to the organization, to the partisan organization.
This is all in the Kovno ghetto.
Yes.
They bring people from French.
I have pictures of them.
From other countries, I don't know.
But I remember one friends.
I was walking with my girlfriend in-- in-- in the street.
And we saw outside in ghetto, with the-- from the [NON-ENGLISH].
How you say in English, [NON-ENGLISH]?
What is-- what is around the ghetto?
Oh the-- from the fence.
From the fence.
And we saw people the same in-- in the yellow--
Stars.
And they ask us, where are you?
We tell them, this is a ghetto.
And they was thinking they came in the ghetto.
But they go-- they didn't took them in the ghetto.
They took them to the nine-- the Fort Nine, where they killed a lot of Jews-- people from Kovno.
This is a special-- a big-- a field.
A-- a field.
And they do-- did the-- dig-- dig--
Digging?
And they-- they-- they put in the-- the people when they kill them, when they shoot them, and where they are in life.
They don't care.
They took the people.
And later on, they put it on the ground of the people.
And--
The ghetto was a big place.
We have different streets.
When the Germans and the Lithuanian polizei, they came in, they cut up a couple streets from right side.
From left side, they do anything to somebody else.
But the people was-- they was in the right side.
They took them in the-- in the buses.
And later, they took them to the fort number nine to kill them.
Nobody knows.
Later, in 1944, a lot of people knows about this place.
The actions, they do this every two months, sometimes two times in the month, when it's came in a Jewish holiday.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
From my family died about 50 people in the action, a lot of friends, a lot of my friends' friends.
It's difficult to tell how many people.
We know the-- first, in the first time, we did not know nothing.
We know in 1943-- this was in-- in-- in July, in August-- we know was the Russian army, they pushed the Germany's army--
--back.
And then the Lithuanians start to help Jewish families.
We hearing about-- the teacher told us in school what is coming the time to be-- fry?
Free.
We don't know.
We don't know that.
I was in the Lithuanian family, and in the evening time, we-- we know.
We was hearing it shooting, and the-- and the-- and the noises from the-- from the-- from the bombs, from everything.
And in the-- in the-- maybe about 6, 7 o'clock, it was a little dark.
And we saw like soldiers, but we don't know what kind of soldiers-- German soldiers, the Russian soldiers.
And this was Russian soldiers came in in this small town where I was.
I was all with this-- my sister, my-- my youngest brother.
And when I go out from ghetto in 1944, I left them in ghetto.
Later, in 1944, this was in March 26, I was not in ghetto.
But I know from my Lithuanian family, they tell me about this terrible things, what the Germans to-- do to the kids in ghetto.
Three days they grab kids from every single place, from every-- from any apartment.
And they killed the kids and the oldest people.
In this meantime, they killed my brother.
He was 13.
And my sister's baby, she was 2 years.
Please what you--
This was in 1944 in March 28-- 26 to 28-- two days.
This was a special-- they do this special to kill kids.
And the-- the rest for the people, they took them in Germany.
My sister, she was-- they-- they took her to the-- to Germany.
She was-- and stood-- and stood over them-- and stood guard.
And I don't know exactly in what place she was.
This--
Yes, this was the-- the beginning when they close the ghetto.
But a lot of people stay in ghetto.
They have bunkers, and they was there.
And the Germans, they know what people, they was--
Hided.
And there was-- fired the ghetto.
And it was a big fire in this mountain.
The ghetto was two weeks--
Burning.
And everybody was there--
Died.
The people that died are-- were the one that were hiding in the bunkers.
Yes.
The other people was in Germany.
They took to Germany.
This was my-- the partisan, the-- the company what-- what they have the special-- a big-- a lot of people were to help kids to survive them.
In Lithuanian family.
After-- after the-- the--
--oh.
After, when I came back to Kovno.
I was from Kovno about six, seven hours to drive with the car.
This was a small town.
This was not like in the city.
This is a small town.
And my Lithuanian family, she bring me to Kovno.
And she find out what-- in the big synagogue-- we have a big synagogue in-- in Kovno-- what people, where they survived, they need to have the names, the-- the people.
And was came back a lot of kids.
And then the people at the organization, the same partisan organization-- they was-- I will tell you about the partisan.
They was go out from ghetto in 1944 in-- before the-- the action of the kids.
And-- and they was fighting with the Germans and the Lithuanian polizei in the [NON-ENGLISH].
In the?
In the forest.
And when it's finished, the war, they came back-- not everybody, but a lot of people came back.
About 200 people was back.
And the organization do what kind peoples came-- survived, except the kids.
And when they have a lot of kids-- about 10 of the 12 kids-- we was thinking that it was a lot of kids.
They open a special kinderheim.
The orphanage.
And little for little, it came back about 30, 40, 50 kids.
Kids was-- was in Russia.
The father and the mother, they was in Kovno.
And-- because this was a big panic.
Nobody knows what to do.
Somebody go on outside, they grab kids, and they took them to different places.
They don't know who kids is that, but they like to help them.
And the kids, when they came back from-- to Kovno, the fathers and the mothers, they was killed.
And the old kids, they hold it in this place to help them.
I was in the-- was there.
And later on, they open a Yiddish school for us.
They're learning us only Yiddish.
And they try to-- to be happy to us.
And they try to tell us, you must be happy.
You survived.
And later we do a lot of things.
We was open songs, clubs, and to play on the piano-- to be happy.
Two years.
When I was in ghetto, when we have the school to learning Lithuanian, we have a couple songs in ghetto too.
In ghetto, the people, they like to remind the people about happiness.
And they do the words what happening in ghetto.
And we was kids.
We grew up like that.
And we would sing songs in the school, in ghetto--
--a lot of songs.
And after the war, we was-- can-- we was-- have a big club.
And we talked to people.
And we explained to people.
And we sung to them a lot of Yiddish songs from ghetto.
